feed.title: Will Eagle
feed.subtitle: Hydrogeologist, Bureau of Economic Geology, UT Austin

entry.title: GSA Connects 2025
entry.content: I had the opportunity this year to present my research at GSA. My poster presentation went really well, and I had a lot of fun saying hi to old friends and meeting new people!
entry.summary: Presenting research and reconnecting with the geoscience community

entry.title: California Tree Coring
entry.content: I ended up helping out some UT folks with a tree coring campaign in the Central Valley of California. They are working on understanding how the critical zone responds to drought using a combination of dendrochronology and rock moisture monitoring! I was excited to be involved and discovered the benefits of power tools in tree coring!
entry.summary: Helping UT researchers study critical zone response to drought in California's Central Valley

entry.title: Summer Adventures
entry.content: After hydro field camp, I spent a month backpacking around Europe! I went to Spain, France, and Italy, and hiked the Tour du Mont Blanc. Definitely a much needed vacation after a hard semester. Lots of cool mountains and plenty of croissants.
entry.summary: Backpacking through Europe after hydro field camp — Spain, France, Italy, and the Tour du Mont Blanc

entry.title: Texas Climate Conference
entry.content: I attended the Texas Climate Conference at Rice University with fellow UT students, where the state’s leading climate scientists came together to talk about the exciting science they are doing. I presented my poster on using tree rings to reconstruct the history of droughts in Texas, and met other young scientists who are passionate about Texas climate!
entry.summary: Presenting drought reconstruction research at Rice University

entry.title: Hot Science — Cool Talks!
entry.content: I’m always excited to help run a “Cool Activity” table before Hot Science - Cool Talks. This time it was about the Future of Texas Water, and I got to share how paleoclimate reconstructions help to inform the use of water resources. It was exciting to see so many people excited to learn about what we are working on at the Banner Research Group!
entry.summary: Running a public outreach table on the Future of Texas Water

entry.title: a post with plotly.js
entry.content:
This is an example post with some plotly code.

```plotly
{
  "data": [
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [10, 15, 13, 17],
      "type": "scatter"
    },
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [16, 5, 11, 9],
      "type": "scatter"
    }
  ]
}
```

Which generates:

{
  "data": [
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [10, 15, 13, 17],
      "type": "scatter"
    },
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [16, 5, 11, 9],
      "type": "scatter"
    }
  ]
}

Also another example chart.

```plotly
{
  "data": [
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [10, 15, 13, 17],
      "mode": "markers"
    },
    {
      "x": [2, 3, 4, 5],
      "y": [16, 5, 11, 9],
      "mode": "lines"
    },
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [12, 9, 15, 12],
      "mode": "lines+markers"
    }
  ],
  "layout": {
    "title": {
      "text": "Line and Scatter Plot"
    }
  }
}
```

This is how it looks like:

{
  "data": [
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [10, 15, 13, 17],
      "mode": "markers"
    },
    {
      "x": [2, 3, 4, 5],
      "y": [16, 5, 11, 9],
      "mode": "lines"
    },
    {
      "x": [1, 2, 3, 4],
      "y": [12, 9, 15, 12],
      "mode": "lines+markers"
    }
  ],
  "layout": {
    "title": {
      "text": "Line and Scatter Plot"
    }
  }
}
entry.summary: this is what included plotly.js code could look like

entry.title: Patagonia!
entry.content: I had the crazy chance (thanks to the Undergraduate Honors Program) to take a trip to Patagonia (Chile and Argentina) with my peers for spring break. It was my first time traveling internationally, and what a great experience it was. Having the opportunity to explore such a geologically unique and naturally beautiful place with some of my best friends, while learning from experts in the field is something I will never forget.
entry.summary: Spring break trip to Chile and Argentina with the Undergraduate Honors Program

entry.title: JSG Research Symposium
entry.content: I had the opportunity to present my research on Texas drought reconstructions at the Jackson School of Geosciences Student Research Symposium. It was a great experience, and I got a lot of feedback on my poster and research. I’m excited to apply what I learned to hone my science communication skills through future presentations!
entry.summary: Presenting Texas drought reconstructions at the Jackson School of Geosciences Student Research Symposium

entry.title: a post with image galleries
entry.content:
The images in this post are all zoomable, arranged into different mini-galleries using different libraries.

Lightbox2

PhotoSwipe

Spotlight JS

Venobox
entry.summary: this is what included image galleries could look like

entry.title: Applied Karst Hydrogeology
entry.content:
This semester I had the pleasure of taking the Applied Karst Hydrogeology class at UT. It was a really fantastic experience and I learned a lot about the complicated resource that is water in the Texas Hill Country.

As a class we went on field trips and collected streamflow, water level, and geophysical data. Each team was responsible for compiling data from one trip into a cohesive report that will be used to inform water resource management for years to come.

Seeing how science, sustainability, policy, and community interact in such a beautiful place was inspiring, and I hope to continue doing such impactful work.
entry.summary: Taking karst hydrogeology in the Texas Hill Country — fieldwork, data collection, and water resource management
